questions.text: Besides annelida and arthropoda the metamerism is exhibited by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Molluscs do not possess metamerism. Metamerism, also known as segmentation, is a characteristic found in some animal groups, like annelids (e.g., earthworms) and arthropods (e.g., insects). Mollusks, including snails, clams, and octopuses, exhibit a different body plan without the segmented structure seen in animals with metamerism.

Acanthocephalans (thorny-headed worms) do not exhibit metamerism or segmentation. Acanthocephalans and cestodes, both belonging to the phylum Platyhelminthes, have a more simplified body structure without distinct segmentation. Their bodies are typically adapted to parasitic lifestyles within their hosts.

Cestodes (tapeworms) do not exhibit metamerism or segmentation. Acanthocephalans and cestodes, both belonging to the phylum Platyhelminthes, have a more simplified body structure without distinct segmentation. Their bodies are typically adapted to parasitic lifestyles within their hosts.

The term 'metamerism' refers to a linear repetition of parts in an animal body. It occurs in three highly organized phyla: Annelida, Arthropoda and Chordata. Each segment is called a metamere, or somite. Segmentation often affects both external and internal structures. Such a condition is called metameric segmentation. In chordates, the segmentation is apparent only in the embryonic stage. In the adult chordates, segmentation is visible in the internal structures, such as vertebrae, ribs, nerves and blood vessels. Other animals have unsegmented bodies.

questions.explanation: The term metamerism refers to the linear repetition of body parts in an animal. It occurs in three highly organized phyla: Annelida, Arthropoda, and Chordata. Each segment is called a metamere or somite. Segmentation often affects both external and internal structures, and this condition is known as metameric segmentation. In chordates, segmentation is clear only in the embryonic stage. In adults, it is visible in internal structures such as vertebrae, ribs, nerves, and blood vessels. Other animals have unsegmented bodies.

questions.text: The organisms attached to the substratum generally possess:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: One single opening to the digestive canal
options.explanation: Echinoderms are usually attached to the substratum and they do not possess single opening in their digestive canal.

options.text: Cilia on the surface to create water current
options.explanation: This is incorrect.As echinoderms doesn't perform this function.

Organisms that are attached to a substratum exhibit radial symmetry in all vertical planes. Animals belonging to Cnidaria (e.g., jellyfish) and Echinodermata (e.g., starfish) are radially symmetrical and are typically sessile in their adult form. In radial symmetry, any plane passing through the central axis divides the body into two identical halves that are mirror images of each other.
Echinoderms usually possess radial symmetry and are not asymmetrical.

questions.explanation: The organisms attached to the substratum possess radial symmetry in all vertical planes. All the animals belonging to cnidaria (e.g. jellyfish) and echinodermata (e.g. starfish) are radially symmetrical and typically sessile in their adult form. In radial symmetry, the parts in an organ or organism, when cut through the centre in any direction, produce two halves that are mirror images of each other.

questions.text: The sympathetic nerves in mammals arise from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These belong to the parasympathetic system, not sympathetic.They control functions like urination, defecation, and sexual reflexes.

These are cranial nerves that form the craniosacral outflow of the parasympathetic system.3rd (oculomotor) → eye muscles7th (facial) → salivary glands9th (glossopharyngeal) → parotid gland10th (vagus) → heart, lungs, digestive organs

The sympathetic nervous system forms a part of autonomic nervous system that consists of nerves which connect the visceral receptors and effectors with the central nervous system through the cranial and spinal nerves. Sympathetic nerves arise from thoracolumbar nerves.
The cervical region mainly contains spinal nerves for the neck and shoulders.Sympathetic fibers may pass through this region but do not originate here.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is a part of the autonomic nervous system. It consists of nerves that connect visceral receptors and effectors with the central nervous system through cranial and spinal nerves. The sympathetic nerves specifically arise from the thoracolumbar region of the spinal cord.

questions.text: A common characteristic of all vertebrates without exception is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The division of body into the head, neck, trunk and tail.
options.explanation: The division of the body into the head, neck, trunk, and tail is a characteristic of animals with a cephalic (head) and caudal (tail) orientation. This is common in vertebrates, including mammals like humans. The head often contains sensory organs and the brain, while the trunk is the main body region, and the tail extends from the posterior end. This body plan provides a clear anatomical organization in these animals. Though this characteristic has exceptions.

options.text: The body covered with an exoskeleton
options.explanation: Vertebrates do not possess exoskeleton.

options.text: The possession of two pairs of functional appendages
options.explanation: This is one of the characteristics of vertebrates but has exceptions.

options.text: The presence of well developed skull
options.explanation: The subphylum Vertebrata, or Craniata, has a well-developed central nervous system that is divided into the brain and spinal cord. The brain is protected by a bony structure called the cranium, which is why these animals are also referred to as Craniata

questions.explanation: The subphylum Vertebrata, or Craniata, has a well-developed central nervous system that is divided into the brain and spinal cord. The brain is protected by a bony structure called the cranium, which is why these animals are also referred to as Craniata

questions.text: One of the special characters of coelenterata only is the occurrence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Polymorphism is exhibited by coelentrates like medusae and obelia but it is not unique to them.
Flame cells are the excretory organs of phylum platyhelminthes i.e: Planaria. They are responsible for the excretion in such organism.
Hermaphroditism is exhibited by coelentrates but it is not unique only to them.

The characteristic cells of coelenterates are stinging cells (cnidocytes, also called cnidoblasts or nematoblasts), which function in offense and defense. When discharged, these cells release a sac called the cnidocyst (or nematocyst), which contains a long thread-like tube. This tube may coil around the prey, attach to it, or inject a toxin called hypnotoxin to paralyze it.

questions.explanation: The characteristic cells of coelenterates are stinging cells (cnidocytes, also called cnidoblasts or nematoblasts), which function in offense and defense. When discharged, these cells release a sac called the cnidocyst (or nematocyst), which contains a long thread-like tube. This tube may coil around the prey, attach to it, or inject a toxin called hypnotoxin to paralyze it.

questions.text: Radial symmetry is usually exhibited in animals which:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Organisms attached to a substratum possess radial symmetry in all vertical planes. Animals belonging to Cnidaria (e.g., jellyfish) and Echinodermata (e.g., starfish) are radially symmetrical and typically sessile in their adult form. In radial symmetry, any plane passing through the central axis divides the body into two mirror-image halves.

options.text: Have one opening of alimentary canal
options.explanation: All the animals belonging to cnidaria (e.g. jellyfish) and echinodermata (e.g. starfish) are radially symmetrical and do not usually possess only one opening in general.

All the animals belonging to cnidaria (e.g. jellyfish) and echinodermata (e.g. starfish) are radially symmetrical and do not usually possess only one opening in general. Not all of them live in water.

All the animals belonging to cnidaria (e.g. jellyfish) and echinodermata (e.g. starfish) are radially symmetrical and do not usually possess only one opening in general. They do not possess cilliary mode of feeding.

questions.explanation: The organisms attached to the substratum possess radial symmetry in all vertical planes. All the animals belonging to cnidaria (e.g. jellyfish) and echinodermata (e.g. starfish) are radially symmetrical and typically sessile in their adult form. In radial symmetry, the parts in an organ or organism, when cut through the centre in any direction, produce two halves that are mirror images of each other.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of platyhelminthes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodium belongs to the phylum Apicomplexa.

Schistosoma, also known as the blood fluke, belongs to the phylum Platyhelminthes and the class Trematoda. These are parasitic flatworms that primarily inhabit the blood vessels of their definitive hosts and are responsible for the disease schistosomiasis in humans
Trypanosoma is a zooflagellate which is animal like protists.
Wuchereria belongs to the phylum Nematoda.

questions.explanation: Platyhelminthes have soft and dorsoventrally flattened body with bilateral symmetry. Plasmodium and Trypanosoma belong to Phylum Protozoa while Wuchereria belongs to Phylum Aschelminthes.

questions.text: Among the following organisms point out a completely non-parasitic form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tape worm is parasitic worm which lives in the intestine of man but also lives in other organisms for some time.
Mosquito is a parasite.

Sea anemone is a completely non-parasitic organism that demonstrates mutualism. It often attaches to the shell of a hermit crab. The sea anemone benefits by feeding on the scraps of food left by the crab and gains mobility as the crab moves. In return, the crab is protected by the stinging cells (nematocysts) in the anemone’s tentacles.
Leech is an ectoparasitic is a segmented worm belonging to the phylum Annelida.

questions.explanation: Sea anemone is a completely non-parasitic form. It shows the example of mutualism. Sea anemone attaches itself to shell of a hermit crab. The anemone obtains nourishment from the scraps of food left by the crab, and is transported from place to place when the crab moves. The crab is protected by the stinging cells in the tentacles of the sea anemone.

questions.text: Tube feet are the characteristic structures of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starfish belongs to the phylum echinodermata who have developed tube feet for locomotion. The tube feet generally protrude out through special radial areas called ambulacra. They are extended and retracted by variations in hydraulic pressure of fluid in them and by contractions of their muscles. Cuttle fish belongs to the Phylum Mollusca and it swims. Crayfish belongs to the Phylum Arthropoda in which locomotion occurs by jointed appendages. Jelly fish belongs to the Phylum Cnidaria and it swims.

Jellyfish, members of the phylum Cnidaria, lack tube feet as well. Cnidarians have a simple body structure characterized by tentacles armed with specialized cells called cnidocytes, used for capturing prey. Tube feet are not a part of their anatomy.

Crayfish, belonging to the phylum Arthropoda, also do not possess tube feet. Arthropods, including crustaceans like crayfish, typically have jointed legs or appendages for movement. Tube feet are a feature more commonly associated with echinoderms.

Cuttlefish do not have tube feet. They belong to the class Cephalopoda, which includes squids, octopuses, and nautiluses. Cephalopods are characterized by a unique set of appendages, such as tentacles and arms, but they lack the tube feet found in echinoderms.

questions.explanation: Starfish belongs to the phylum echinodermata who have developed tube feet for locomotion. The tube feet generally protrude out through special radial areas called ambulacra. They are extended and retracted by variations in hydraulic pressure of fluid in them and by contractions of their muscles. Cuttle fish belongs to the Phylum Mollusca and it swims. Crayfish belongs to the Phylum Arthropoda in which locomotion occurs by jointed appendages. Jelly fish belongs to the Phylum Cnidaria and it swims.

questions.text: Which of the following does not have an open circulatory system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In frogs, including the tadpole stage, the circulatory system undergoes significant changes during metamorphosis. Tadpoles have a simpler circulatory system adapted for aquatic life, while adult frogs have a more complex system suitable for both aquatic and terrestrial environments.

Prawns have an open circulatory system as well. Their circulatory system involves a tubular heart that pumps hemolymph into the body cavity, and then it returns to the heart through openings called ostia.

Chelifer, being an arachnid (related to spiders and scorpions), does not have a circulatory system with blood vessels. Instead, they have an open circulatory system called hemolymph, where the blood-like fluid bathes the organs directly.

Cockroaches also have an open circulatory system. Their circulatory system consists of a tubular heart that pumps hemolymph into the body cavity, where it bathes the organs directly. The hemolymph then re-enters the heart through openings called ostia.

questions.explanation: In the open circulatory system, the blood is not confined to the blood vessels, but it flows in the open spaces. Prawn, Chelifer and cockroach have open circulatory systems. Frog’s tadpole has a closed circulatory system, that is the blood flows in the blood vessels.

questions.text: The ostrich, penguin and kiwi are _ .
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Running birds, or ratites, are flightless birds adapted for terrestrial life, often with strong legs for running. However, this category doesn't include all of the birds mentioned.
Migratory birds travel between breeding and wintering grounds. None of the listed birds are known for regular migratory patterns.
The ostrich, penguin, and kiwi are all flightless birds, each adapted to different environments: ostriches for running, penguins for swimming, and kiwis for ground foraging.
While some birds have different toe arrangements, the term 'four-toed' is misleading for the birds listed, as they are not defined by their toe configuration.

questions.explanation: The ostrich, penguin, and kiwi are all flightless birds. This category best captures their shared trait of being unable to fly, though they have adapted to different environments: the ostrich to running on land, the penguin to swimming in water, and the kiwi to foraging on the ground. Other options are incorrect because they either apply to only one of the birds or are not characteristic of any of them.

questions.text: Which of the following assists in locomotion?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trichocysts are small, elongated, bottle-shaped structures found in the cytoplasm of certain protozoa, including Paramecium. Paramecium is a unicellular, ciliated organism belonging to the phylum Ciliophora.
Pedicellariae are small, pincer-like structures found on the surface of echinoderms, including starfish (or sea stars). These structures play a protective and cleaning role for the starfish.

The clitellum is a prominent, swollen, and glandular section in the body of earthworms, including Pheretima (a genus of earthworms). It is a reproductive structure involved in the formation of egg capsules and the exchange of sperm during copulation. The clitellum is usually located near the anterior end of the earthworm’s body.
The looping or crawling movement in Hirudinaria is performed with the help of muscles and suckers which serve for attachment.

questions.explanation: The looping or crawling movement in Hirudinaria is performed with the help of muscles and suckers which serve for attachment.

questions.text: What is true about Taenia saginata?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Life history has pig as intermediate host
options.explanation: The pig is the intermediate host of Taenia solium (the pork tapeworm), not Taenia saginata.Taenia saginata (the beef tapeworm) has the cow (cattle) as its intermediate host.Humans are the definitive hosts in both cases.

options.text: There are two large suckers on scolex
options.explanation: The scolex (head) of Taenia saginata has four suckers, not two.These suckers are muscular and help the tapeworm attach to the intestinal wall.

The beef tapeworm Taenia saginata is similar to the pork tapeworm Taenia solium, in structure and life history. It is the commonest tapeworm of man with a much greater incidence than that of T. solium. Scolex bears four strong, rounded, adhesive suckers but lacks hooks and rostellum.

options.text: Rostellum has double circle of hooks
options.explanation: This feature belongs to Taenia solium, not Taenia saginata.T. solium has a rostellum with two rows (a double circle) of hooks.T. saginata has no hooks or rostellum at all.

questions.explanation: The beef tapeworm Taenia saginata is similar to the pork tapeworm Taenia solium, in structure and life history. It is the commonest tapeworm of man with a much greater incidence than that of T. solium. Scolex bears four strong, rounded, adhesive suckers but lacks hooks and rostellum.

questions.text: Which one of the following animals possesses nerve cells but no nerves?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydra, belonging to the phylum Cnidaria, possesses a nerve net without centralized nerves. The nerve net allows for the coordination of simple responses to stimuli, such as touch or light changes.
Tapeworms are members of the phylum Platyhelminthes and have a rudimentary nervous system called a nerve ladder, composed of ganglia and nerve cords.
Earthworms, from the phylum Annelida, have a segmented nervous system with ganglia in each segment connected by a ventral nerve cord, allowing for more advanced movement and response coordination.
Frog tadpoles have a brain and spinal cord, which develops further during metamorphosis into an adult frog, resulting in a more complex central nervous system.

questions.explanation: Hydra, belonging to the phylum Cnidaria, possesses a nerve net without centralized nerves. The nerve net allows for the coordination of simple responses to stimuli, such as touch or light changes.

questions.text: Budding is a normal mode of asexual reproduction in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starfish can reproduce asexually through fragmentation. A broken-off part, usually an arm with a portion of the central disk, can regenerate into a new starfish. Hydra reproduces by budding.
Budding is an asexual mode of reproduction in Hydra and sponges. Buds are formed as outgrowths on the body surface and then detached to form new animals
Some tapeworms can undergo asexual reproduction called fission. The body may split into fragments, each capable of developing into a new tapeworm. Hydra reproduces by budding.
Starfish can reproduce asexually through fragmentation. A broken-off part, usually an arm with a portion of the central disk, can regenerate into a new starfish. Sponges reproduce by budding.

questions.explanation: Budding is an asexual mode of reproduction in Hydra and sponges. Buds are formed as outgrowths on the body surface and then detached to form new animals.

questions.text: Tracheae of cockroach and mammal are similar in having:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trachea is not paired in mammals and cockroach.

Tracheae act as passage of air during respiration in both cockroach and mammals. In cockroach, the cuticular lining is spirally thickened forming taenidia which prevents the tracheal tubes from collapsing. In mammals, cartilaginous rings supporting the walls of the tracheae prevent their collapsing.
Unlike cockroaches, trachea in man possesses ciliated inner lining
Trachea in man does not originated from head.

questions.explanation: Tracheae act as passage of air during respiration in both cockroach and mammals. In cockroach, the cuticular lining is spirally thickened forming taenidia which prevents the tracheal tubes from collapsing. In mammals, cartilaginous rings supporting the walls of the tracheae prevent their collapsing.

questions.text: A larval stage occurs in the life history of all members of which option?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Larval stage doesn't occur in all of them.Frog → has a larval stage called tadpole Lizard → NO larval stage, it hatches as a miniature adult Cockroach → NO larval stage, instead it has nymphs (incomplete metamorphosis) Incorrect because only frog has a true larval stage.

Ascaris (roundworm) → develops directly; no larva stage in humans. Housefly → has a larval stage called maggot Frog → larval stage is tadpole Only two (housefly and frog) have larvae → not all three, so wrong.
Housefly → larva = maggot Earthworm → no larval stage, develops directly Mosquito → larva = wriggler Earthworm lacks a larval stage→ wrong.
In butterfly, the larval stage is known as catterpillar, in frog it is known as tadpole, while in mosquito it is known as wriggler.

questions.explanation: In butterfly, the larval stage is known as catterpillar, in frog it is known as tadpole, while in mosquito it is known as wriggler.

questions.text: Gorillas, chimpanzees, monkeys and humans belong to the same:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A species is the basic unit of biological classification. It consists of individuals that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring under natural conditions.
A genus is a taxonomic rank in the biological classification of living organisms. It groups species that are closely related and share common characteristics.
Family is a higher taxonomic rank than genus and species. It groups together related genera that share broader similarities.

Gorillas, chimpanzees, monkeys and humans belong to the same order i.e., primates. They have well developed brain flat nails on fingers and toes. First digit is usually opposable, an adaptation for grasping. Eyes are typically large and turned forward

questions.explanation: Gorillas, chimpanzees, monkeys and humans belong to the same order i.e., primates. They have well developed brain flat nails on fingers and toes. First digit is usually opposable, an adaptation for grasping. Eyes are typically large and turned forward

questions.text: What is common in whale, bat and rat?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Absence of neck is not common to whale, bat and rat.

options.text: Muscular diaphragm between thorax and abdomen
options.explanation: Whale, bat and rat are mammals. Diaphragm is present in mammals. The diaphragm separates the thoracic cavity (with lung and heart) from the abdominal cavity (with digestive system and urogenital system). In its relaxed state, the diaphragm is shaped like a dome. It is controlled by the phrenic nerve.

options.text: Extraabdominal testes to avoid high temperature of body
options.explanation: This is not a common characteristic of whale, bat and rat.
This is not a characteristic of mammals.

questions.explanation: Whale, bat and rat are mammals. Diaphragm is present in mammals. The diaphragm separates the thoracic cavity (with lung and heart) from the abdominal cavity (with digestive system and urogenital system). In its relaxed state, the diaphragm is shaped like a dome. It is controlled by the phrenic nerve.

questions.text: The bullfrog of India is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bullfrog of india is common name of Rana tigrina.
"Rana sylvatica” is the scientific name for the wood frog.
“R. catesbeiana” is the scientific name for the American bullfrog.
"R. esculenta” is the scientific name for the Edible Frog.

questions.explanation: The common Indian bull frog Rana tigrina lives in or near permanent freshwater lakes, ponds and streams. It is in the water most of the time. It lives near water mainly for two reasons: (i) To keep skin moist to continue with cutaneous respiration, and (ii) To immediately jump or slip into wate to escape from enemies.

questions.text: Aristotle’s lantern occurs in which class?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aristotle’s lantern is a complex chewing apparatus found in sea urchins, which belong to the class Echinoidea. This structure aids in the feeding process by allowing the sea urchin to scrape and chew food.
Asteroidea, the class of echinoderms including starfish or sea stars, do not possess Aristotle's lantern. They have a different feeding mechanism involving everting their stomach over their prey.
Holothuroidea includes sea cucumbers, which have a soft body and feed using tentacles around their mouth, lacking the complex structure of Aristotle's lantern.
Ophiuroidea, comprising brittle stars and basket stars, do not have Aristotle's lantern. They feed using their arms to capture food particles.

questions.explanation: Aristotle’s lantern occurs in the Class Echinoidea. Five teeth surrounding the mouth are attached to a masticatory apparatus call Aristotle’s lantern. It is named Aristiotle's lantern after its discoverer and because it resembles an ancient Greek ship lantern. It is situated within the test and projects slightly through the mouth. It consists of five large calcareous plates, called pyramids or alveoli. By means of special protractor and retractor muscles, the lantern can be partially protracted and retracted through the mouth. Aristotle’s lantern is used in feeding.

questions.text: Which class do starfish belong to?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starfish belong to the Class Asteroidea, characterized by the presence of five or more arms not sharply set off from a central disc. They are free living marine animals that occur on sandy or muddy bottoms and crawl about over rocks and shells. All are carnivorous, feeding mainly on crustaceans, polychaetes and molluscs. They also feed on detritus and plankton. They generally exhibit remarkable powers of autotomy and regeneration.

This class includes brittle stars and basket stars, not true starfish.They have slender, flexible arms that are clearly distinct from the central disc, unlike starfish.So, not starfish but brittle stars.
This class includes sea cucumbers, which are elongated and soft-bodied, not star-shaped.They crawl on the sea floor and have no arms. So, not starfish.
This class includes sea lilies and feather stars.They have many feathery arms and are often attached to the sea bottom by a stalk (especially sea lilies).

questions.explanation: Starfish belong to the Class Asteroidea, characterized by the presence of five or more arms not sharply set off from a central disc. They are free living marine animals that occur on sandy or muddy bottoms and crawl about over rocks and shells. All are carnivorous, feeding mainly on crustaceans, polychaetes and molluscs. They also feed on detritus and plankton. They generally exhibit remarkable powers of autotomy and regeneration.

questions.text: Eye of the molluscan group that resembles vertebrate eye is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This molluscan group does not have eye resembling to vertebrat eye.
This molluscan group does not have eye resembling to vertebrat eye.
This molluscan group does not have eye resembling to vertebrat eye.

In cephalopodas, paired eyes are large, efficient and bulge from the dorsolateral sides of the head. They bear striking resemblance to those of a vertebrate because a cornea, iris, lens and retina are present. Their lens projects an inverted image on the retina just like the vertebrate eye. External muscle attachments enable limited movements of the eye. But the embryological development of the cephalopod eye is entirely different from that of the vertebrate eye, and hence they areo homologically different. The vertebrate eye is formed as an outgrowth of the brain, while the cephalopod eye is formed by an ectodermal invagination.

questions.explanation: In cephalopodas, paired eyes are large, efficient and bulge from the dorsolateral sides of the head. They bear striking resemblance to those of a vertebrate because a cornea, iris, lens and retina are present. Their lens projects an inverted image on the retina just like the vertebrate eye. External muscle attachments enable limited movements of the eye. But the embryological development of the cephalopod eye is entirely different from that of the vertebrate eye, and hence they areo homologically different. The vertebrate eye is formed as an outgrowth of the brain, while the cephalopod eye is formed by an ectodermal invagination.

questions.text: The adult Culex and Anopheles can be distinguished with the help of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Both Culex and Anopheles have similar mouth parts adapted for piercing and sucking blood.There is no major difference in their mouthpart structure or coloration that can be easily used to distinguish them.Color differences are not reliable for identification. So, not mouth parts or color.

Two common mosquito genera, the Anopheles and Culex can be easily identified by their sitting postures. When sitting, the abdomen of Anopheles is always held at an angle to the surface while that of Culex is held parallel to the surface.

While male and female mosquitoes can be distinguished by their antennae (males are bushy, females are not),this does not differentiate between Culex and Anopheles.Both genera have scaled wings, and wing differences are minor — not useful for simple identification.

Both Culex and Anopheles feed on blood (females only) and nectar (males).The main difference lies in the type of disease they transmit, not in feeding habits:Anopheles → MalariaCulex → Filariasis, Encephalitis

questions.explanation: Two common mosquito genera, the Anopheles and Culex can be easily identified by their sitting postures. When sitting, the abdomen of Anopheles is always held at an angle to the surface while that of Culex is held parallel to the surface.

questions.text: Sound box of birds is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A pygostyle is a bony structure found in the tails of some birds. It is a fused terminal section of the vertebral column and is formed by the fusion of several caudal (tail) vertebrae.

In birds, the larynx is a specialized structure located at the base of the trachea, where it connects to the bronchi. The avian larynx serves several important functions related to vocalization and respiration
Syrinx is the voice organ of birds.
The synsacrum is a skeletal feature found in birds, particularly in the pelvic region. It is the fusion of the pelvic vertebrae with the sacral vertebrae, forming a single structure.

questions.explanation: Syrinx is the voice organ of birds.

questions.text: What is the Ascaris larva called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A cysticercus is the larval form of certain tapeworms, belonging to the family Taeniidae

In Ascaris, the rhabditiform larva in the first stage is not infective. In a week’s time, it moults within the egg shell and becomes a second stage rhabditoid which is capable of infecting the host. Cysticercus, hexacanth and onchosphere are the larval stages of Taenia.
A hexacanth refers to the larval stage of certain tapeworms, specifically to the characteristic structure found in the early larval form called the oncosphere.
A hexacanth refers to the larval stage of certain tapeworms, specifically to the characteristic structure found in the early larval form called the oncosphere.

questions.explanation: In Ascaris, the rhabditiform larva in the first stage is not infective. In a week’s time, it moults within the egg shell and becomes a second stage rhabditoid which is capable of infecting the host. Cysticercus, hexacanth and onchosphere are the larval stages of Taenia.

questions.text: What is correct about Taenia?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Male organs occur in posterior proglottides
options.explanation: In posterior (gravid) proglottides, male organs degenerate after fertilization.Only the uterus full of eggs remains. So, male organs are not in the posterior region.

options.text: Male organs occur in anterior proglottides
options.explanation: The anterior (immature) proglottides do not have fully formed reproductive organs yet — they are still developing. So, male organs are not fully formed in the anterior region.

options.text: Female organs occur in anterior proglottides
options.explanation: Same reason anterior proglottides are immature and lack functional organs (both male and female.

options.text: Mature proglottides contain both male and female organs
options.explanation: There are about 450 mature proglottids forming the middle part of strobila. These are large and squarish in outline. The anterior 100 to 150 proglottids contain only male reproductive organs, while the posterior 250 mature proglottids develop both male and female reproductive organs making them hermaphrodite.

questions.explanation: There are about 450 mature proglottids forming the middle part of strobila. These are large and squarish in outline. The anterior 100 to 150 proglottids contain only male reproductive organs, while the posterior 250 mature proglottids develop both male and female reproductive organs making them hermaphrodite.

questions.text: The simplest type of canal system in Porifera is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sponges belong to the Phylum Porifera. Ostia, spongocoel and osculum together form a canal system which is characteristic of all sponges. Canal system of Leucosolenia is of ascon type. It is the simplest type of canal system found in sponges. Water enters directly through ostia into the central spongocoel, which is lined by choanocytes, and leaves through osculum. Sycon type of canal system is found in Sycons and Leucon type is found in Spongilla. There is no canal system named as a radial type.

This is the most complex type, not the simplest.Found in advanced sponges like Euspongia.It has many small flagellated chambers and no central spongocoel.Water passes through many canals before leaving. So, most complex, not simplest.

This is intermediate between Ascon and Leucon.Found in sponges like Scypha (or Sycon).The body wall is folded, forming radial canals lined with choanocytes more complex than Ascon type. So, not the simplest, but moderately complex.

The term “radial type” refers to the radial symmetry of some sponges, not to a canal system.The correct recognized canal system types are Ascon, Sycon, and Leucon only.So, “radial type” is not a valid canal system classification.

questions.explanation: Sponges belong to the Phylum Porifera. Ostia, spongocoel and osculum together form a canal system which is characteristic of all sponges. Canal system of Leucosolenia is of ascon type. It is the simplest type of canal system found in sponges. Water enters directly through ostia into the central spongocoel, which is lined by choanocytes, and leaves through osculum. Sycon type of canal system is found in Sycons and Leucon type is found in Spongilla. There is no canal system named as a radial type.

questions.text: What is an example of an egg laying mammal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Kangroo is a marsupial mammal. kangaroos give birth to live young. Female kangaroos have a pouch in which they carry and nurse their undeveloped offspring, known as joeys.

The Duckbilled platypus is an egg laying mammal. It is found in the rivers in eastern Australia and Tasmania. It is a beaver like monotreme about 5060 cm long and well adapted to live in water. Usually, two eggs are laid at a time. The female curls around them for incubation and remains inactive for about two weeks. Newly hatched young ones are very immature, naked, blind and only 2.5 cm long.
Koalas, like all other mammals, give birth to live young. They are marsupials, which means that they carry their underdeveloped young in a pouch after birth.

Whales, unlike marsupials, are placental mammals. Placental mammals carry their young internally, nourishing them through a placenta until they are more developed and capable of surviving outside the womb.

questions.explanation: Platypus is a monotreme, one of the only egg-laying mammals along with the echidna. Kangaroo, koala, and whale are all viviparous mammals that give birth to live young.

questions.text: Kidney of adult rabbit is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pronephros is the embryonic kidney structure that forms early in vertebrate development but is non-functional in most species, including mammals. It is a transient structure that precedes the development of the mesonephros.

The kidney of an adult rabbit is a metanephros. In the development of vertebrate kidneys, the metanephros is the final and functional form. It is the permanent kidney structure found in adult mammals, including rabbits.

The mesonephros is the second stage in the development of vertebrate kidneys. It becomes functional during embryonic development and serves as the temporary excretory organ in fish and amphibians. In mammals, including rabbits, the mesonephros is largely replaced by the metanephros during development.

Opisthonephros is a term used in some vertebrates, particularly certain fish, to describe a posteriorly located kidney structure. In the context of mammalian kidney development, it is not commonly used, and the term metanephros is more relevant.

questions.explanation: Kidney of adult rabbit is metanephros. It is formed from the posterior end of the nephrogenic mesoderm which is displaced somewhat anteriorly and laterally.

questions.text: Which one occurs in echinodermata?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Larval forms of echinoderms have bilateral symmetry.Found in animals like humans, insects, and worms which can be divided into two equal halves (left and right).Echinoderms are bilateral only in their larval stage, but adults are radial, so this option is incorrect for adult echinoderms.

Adult echinoderms have radial symmetry.In radial symmetry, the body parts are arranged around a central axis like spokes around a wheel.Echinoderms (e.g., starfish) can be divided into similar halves by multiple planes passing through the center.They usually show pentamerous (five-part) radial symmetry meaning their body is arranged in fives (like five arms of a starfish).
A porous body (body with tiny pores) is a feature of Porifera (sponges), not echinoderms.Echinoderms have a hard, calcareous endoskeleton, not a porous body wall.
Echinoderms have a hard, spiny skin made of calcium plates (ossicles) that’s why they’re called “spiny-skinned animals.”So, they do not have soft skin.

questions.explanation: Radial symmetry is the arrangement of parts in an organ or organism such that cutting through the centre of the structure in any direction produces two halves that are mirror images of each other. All animals belonging to the cnidaria (e.g., jellyfish) and adult echinodermata (e.g., starfish) are radially symmetrical.

questions.text: An insect regarded as greatest the mechanical carrier of diseases is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a parasitic insect that feeds on human blood.It transmits diseases like typhus and relapsing fever, but this happens biologically germs multiply inside the louse before transmission. So, Pediculus is a biological vector, not a mechanical carrier.
They are not greatest carriers of diseases.It is a blood-sucking ectoparasite, but it does not transmit diseases effectively.It causes itching and irritation, not major disease transmission.

The insect regarded as the greatest mechanical carrier of diseases is the housefly (Musca domestica). Houseflies are known for their close association with human habitations, where they can pick up pathogens from various sources, including feces, garbage, and contaminated food. They can then transfer these pathogens to human food and surfaces, contributing to the spread of diseases such as diarrhea and food poisoning. Proper sanitation and hygiene practices are crucial in minimizing the health risks associated with houseflies.

Xenopsylla cheopis = rat fleaIt is the biological vector of plague (Yersinia pestis).The bacteria multiply inside the flea before transmission — this is biological, not mechanical. So, Xenopsylla is not a mechanical carrier.

questions.explanation: Musca is the zoological name of the house fly which is regarded as the mechanical carrier of many diseases. It is very active and keeps on visiting on dirty things and eatables as well.

questions.text: Metamorphosis of insects is regulated through which hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pheromones are chemicals used for communication between individuals of the same species, often for mating or signaling danger, but they do not regulate metamorphosis.
Thyroxine is primarily associated with vertebrates, especially in regulating metabolism, and is not involved in insect metamorphosis.
Ecdysone is the key hormone that regulates molting and metamorphosis in insects. It triggers developmental changes by stimulating the shedding of the old exoskeleton.
This option is incorrect as only ecdysone is responsible for regulating insect metamorphosis.

questions.explanation: Ecdysone is a steroid hormone, secreted by a pair of prothoracic glands in the thorax of insects and by Yorgans in crustaceans, that stimulates moulting and metamorphosis. In insects, its release is stimulated by prothoracicotropic hormone.

questions.text: Classification of Porifera is based on
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They are not classifies with respect to branching.

The Phylum Porifera is divided into three classes on the basis of spicules (skeleton) : calcarea or calcispongiae, hexactinellida or hyalospongiae and demospongiae or sclerospongiae. Class Calcarea have calcareous spicules, Class Hexactinellida have siliceous spicules and Class Demospongiae have siliceous spicules or spongin fibres.
Reproduction is not responsible for classification of phylum porifera.
Members of phylum porifera lack any kind of symmetry and are asymmetrical.

questions.explanation: The Phylum Porifera is divided into three classes on the basis of spicules (skeleton) : calcarea or calcispongiae, hexactinellida or hyalospongiae and demospongiae or sclerospongiae. Class Calcarea have calcareous spicules, Class Hexactinellida have siliceous spicules and Class Demospongiae have siliceous spicules or spongin fibres.

questions.text: The excretory structures of flatworms/Taenia are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Flame cells are scattered throughout the parenchyma from which they remove metabolic wastes. A flame cell is of irregular shape, with granular cytoplasm and a nucleus. The bundle of cilia, or flame, arises from basal granules near the nucleus. Cilia are enclosed into a funnel shaped lumen formed by the terminal blind end of a capillary.

Protonephridia are found in flatworms.The whole excretory system made up of flame cells and tubules is called a protonephridial system.So, technically:Flame cells = individual excretory cellsProtonephridia = entire network/system
Malpighian tubules are found in insects.Function: Remove nitrogenous waste (uric acid) from hemolymph.
Green glands are found in crustaceans. Function: Excretion of nitrogenous waste.

questions.explanation: Flatworms like Taenia have flame cells, which are specialized excretory cells. They are part of the protonephridial system, helping in osmoregulation and removal of waste.

questions.text: Bladderworm/cysticercus is the larval stage of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cysticercus is the larval stage of tapeworm which is characterised by a large vesicle and one scolex. Cysticercus develops in adult tapeworm only when ingested by the human host. In the pig’s body it leads quite an inactive life and remains viable for several years, after which it dies and becomes calcified. Pork (pig’s flesh) containing viable cysticerci is called measly pork because of its spotted appearance.

Roundworms are parasites. They use the human body to stay alive, feed and reproduce. If a lot of eggs have been swallowed, or if the worms move from the small intestine to other parts of the body, they can cause serious complications, such as a bowel obstruction.
The pinworm, also known as threadworm or seatworm, is a parasitic worm. It is a nematode and a common intestinal parasite or helminth, especially in humans.
Liver fluke is a collective name of a polyphyletic group of parasitic trematodes under the phylum Platyhelminthes. They are principally parasites of the liver of various mammals, including humans.

questions.explanation: Cysticercus is the larval stage of tapeworm which is characterised by a large vesicle and one scolex. Cysticercus develops in adult tapeworm only when ingested by the human host. In the pig’s body it leads quite an inactive life and remains viable for several years, after which it dies and becomes calcified. Pork (pig’s flesh) containing viable cysticerci is called measly pork because of its spotted appearance.

questions.text: Ecdysis refers to the shedding of the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is correct. The stratum corneum is the outermost layer of the epidermis, composed mainly of dead cells. In reptiles, it is shed during ecdysis, allowing for growth.
This is incorrect. The epidermis is the outer skin layer but not specifically the layer shed during ecdysis.
This is incorrect. The dermis is the layer beneath the epidermis and is not involved in ecdysis.
This is incorrect. The stratum malpighi, or stratum basale, is a deeper layer of the epidermis responsible for cell regeneration, not ecdysis.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the stratum corneum, which is the outermost layer of the epidermis. It consists of dead cells and is shed during ecdysis in reptiles. This process allows for growth and the removal of old, worn-out skin. The other options are incorrect because they refer to either deeper skin layers or do not relate to the process of ecdysis directly.

questions.text: Penguins are usually present in _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Australia does have one small species, the Little Blue Penguin, but penguins are not usually found across the continent — they are rare and limited to coastal regions.So, not their main or usual habitat.

Penguins are a group of aquatic, flightless birds living almost exclusively in Antarctica. All penguins are countershaded, i.e they have a white underside and a dark (mostly black) upperside. This is for camouflage.

The African Penguin (also called the Jackass Penguin) lives along the southern coast of Africa, but it is only one species among many. Penguins are not common or "usually present" in Africa only a few coastal populations exist.

Some penguins (like the Galápagos Penguin) live near South America, but these are exceptions.The majority of penguin species are found much farther south. So, not the usual or main region for penguins.

questions.explanation: Penguins are a group of aquatic, flightless birds living almost exclusively in Antarctica. All penguins are countershaded, i.e they have a white underside and a dark (mostly black) upperside. This is for camouflage.

questions.text: Kala azar and Oriental Sores are spread by the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Housefly is not responsible for such sores.
Beg bugs are not responsible for kala azar and oriental sores.

Visceral leishmaniasis, also known as kala azar and black fever, is the most severe form of leishmaniasis, a disease caused by parasites of the Leishmania genus. It is transmitted by the sand fly. The adult female sand fly is a bloodsucker, usually feeding at night on sleeping prey. When the fly bites an animal infected with L. donovani, the pathogen is ingested along with the prey's blood. Leishmania tropica produces skin ulcers known as oriental sores or Delhi sores. The disease is spread by sand flies. The parasite lives in the endothelial cells of skin capillaries. It leads to ulcerated wounds with raised edges which do not cause much pain.
Fruit fly are not responsible for such sores.

questions.explanation: Visceral leishmaniasis, also known as kala azar and black fever, is the most severe form of leishmaniasis, a disease caused by parasites of the Leishmania genus. The disease is spread by sand flies. The parasite lives in the endothelial cells of skin capillaries. It leads to ulcerated wounds with raised edges which do not cause much pain.

questions.text: Malpighian tubules are the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In insects Malpighian tubules are attached to the alimentary canal at the extreme anterior end of hindgut. These are fine, long, unbranched, yellowish and blind tubules lying freely in the haemolymph. These are between 60 to 150 in number and are arranged in 68 bundles. They excrete out nitrogenous wastes from the body in the form of uric acid.
Annelids, including earthworms, have a pair of nephridia as their excretory organs. Nephridia are tubular structures responsible for removing metabolic waste, excess fluids, and salts from the body.
Insects have a respiratory system consisting of small tubes called tracheae that deliver air directly to the cells.

Annelids, which are segmented worms, typically respire through their moist skin. While they lack specialized respiratory organs like lungs or gills, their skin allows for the exchange of gases, such as oxygen and carbon dioxide, with the environment.

These are fine, long, unbranched, yellowish and blind tubules lying freely in the haemolymph. These are between 60 to 150 in number and are arranged in 68 bundles. They excrete out nitrogenous wastes from the body in the form of uric acid.

questions.text: Taenia saginata differs from Taenia solium because of the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The beef tapeworm Taenia saginata is similar to the pork tapeworm Taenia solium, in structure and life history. It is the commonest tapeworm of man with a much greater incidence than that of T. solium. Its intermediate hosts are cattle and buffaloes. It is longer than T.solium, usually attaining a length upto 12 meters or more. Scolex bears four strong, rounded, adhesive suckers but lacks hooks. Strobila comprises of upto 2,000 proglottids. A gravid proglottid contains about 100,000 eggs. Uterus of gravid proglottids has 15 to 35 branches on either side.

options.text: Absence of scolex hooks and uterine branching
options.explanation: T. saginata does not lack uterine branching — in fact, it has more branches than T. solium.T. saginata: 15–30 lateral uterine branches on each side.T. solium: 7–13 branches on each side. So, T. saginata does not lack uterine branching; it just has more of them.

options.text: Absence of scolex hooks and presence of both male and female reproductive organs
options.explanation: Both T. saginata and T. solium are hermaphroditic, meaning they both have male and female reproductive organs in each mature proglottid. So, this feature does not distinguish between them.
The presence of scolex hooks is a feature of T. solium, not T. saginata. So, this statement is the opposite of the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The beef tapeworm Taenia saginata is similar to the pork tapeworm Taenia solium, in structure and life history. It is the commonest tapeworm of man with a much greater incidence than that of T. solium. Its intermediate hosts are cattle and buffaloes. It is longer than T.solium, usually attaining a length upto 12 meters or more. Scolex bears four strong, rounded, adhesive suckers but lacks hooks. Strobila comprises of upto 2,000 proglottids. A gravid proglottid contains about 100,000 eggs. Uterus of gravid proglottids has 15 to 35 branches on either side.

questions.text: Eutherians are characterised by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All mammals have hairy skin as a characteristic feature. Hair is a defining characteristic of the class Mammalia, which includes a wide variety of species ranging from rodents and bats to whales and humans.

Eutheria is a taxon containing the placental mammals, such as humans. Nevertheless, all Eutherians are placental mammals. This means that a Eutherian foetus is nourished during gestation by a placenta. Eutherians are also viviparous, meaning that the offspring are carried in the mother's womb until fully developed.
Ovoviviparity is a reproductive mode found in some mammals, including certain species within the class Mammalia.
This is incorrect. Eutherians don't have glandular skin.

questions.explanation: Eutheria is a taxon containing the placental mammals, such as humans. Nevertheless, all Eutherians are placental mammals. This means that a Eutherian foetus is nourished during gestation by a placenta. Eutherians are also viviparous, meaning that the offspring are carried in the mother's womb until fully developed.

questions.text: The wishbone of birds comes from the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Wishbone arises from pectoral girdle not pelvic.
Skull is not related to wishbone in birds.
Wishbone does not arise from hindlimbs.

The wishbone, known in anatomy as the furcula, is a sternum bone found in birds which is shaped like the letter Y. It is used as an attachment point for the wing muscles. It is so named because of a tradition:Two people pull on each side of such a bone, and when it breaks, the one who gets the larger part is said to have a wish granted. The fork shaped bone is formed from wo clavicles fused with the inter clavicle.

questions.explanation: The wishbone, known in anatomy as the furcula, is a sternum bone found in birds which is shaped like the letter Y. It is used as an attachment point for the wing muscles. The fork shaped bone is formed from wo clavicles fused with the inter clavicle.

questions.text: Flight muscles of bird are attached to the _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Flight muscles are connected to keel of sternum.The clavicles (collarbones) of birds fuse to form the furcula (wishbone).The furcula acts like a spring during wing movement — it helps store and release energy, but flight muscles are not attached here directly.
In birds, the pectoral and supracoracoideus muscles that power the wings are anchored to a large bony keel along the midline of the sternum.

The scapula (shoulder blade) forms part of the shoulder girdle and gives support to the wing joint,but only a few small muscles are attached here.The main flight muscles are attached to the keel, not the scapula.

The coracoid bone helps strengthen the shoulder girdle and prevents collapse during wing flapping.It connects the sternum to the shoulder joint, but major flight muscles are not attached to it. It supports the structure, not the flight muscle attachment.

questions.explanation: In birds, the pectoral and supracoracoideus muscles that power the wings are anchored to a large bony keel along the midline of the sternum.

questions.text: Which option describes one of the characters of a chordate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gills are not present in fully developed forms of all chordates.
This is incorrect. Chordates don't have spiracles.

The diagnostic characters of chordates are notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits and a post anal tail. The tail is the part of the body behind the cloacal or anal opening. It contains skeletal elements, muscles, blood vessels and nerves but no viscera. It provides much of the propulsive force in aquatic species. The tail is reduced or absent in the adults of some chordates.
Chitinous exoskeleton is a characteristi of arthropods. Mammals possess endoskeleton.

questions.explanation: The diagnostic characters of chordates are notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits and a post anal tail. The tail is the part of the body behind the cloacal or anal opening. It contains skeletal elements, muscles, blood vessels and nerves but no viscera. It provides much of the propulsive force in aquatic species. The tail is reduced or absent in the adults of some chordates.

questions.text: Earthworms are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Earthworms are very useful. All over the world they are used as bait for fishing. Earthworms are in general beneficial to agriculture. Their habit of burrowing and swallowing earth increases fertility of soil in many ways. Their burrows permit penetration of air and moisture in porous soil, improve drainage, and make easier the downward growth of roots. Excretory wastes and other secretions of worms also enrich soil by adding nitrogenous matters that form important plant food. Earthworms were also used as medicines in the past. They were used to cure stones in the bladder, the yellowness of jaundice, pyorrhoea, piles, rheumatism, gout and diarrhoea. Earthworms are easily obtained and are of convenient size for dissections. They are, therefore, universally employed for class studies and for investigations in general and comparative physiology.
Use of earthworms is useful, not harmful.

Yes, earthworms are generally more useful than harmful. They contribute to soil aeration, nutrient cycling, and overall soil health. However, introducing non-native earthworm species can have negative impacts on ecosystems, so caution is advised.
Earthworms are not more harmful. They are instead more useful.

They were used to cure stones in the bladder, the yellowness of jaundice, pyorrhoea, piles, rheumatism, gout and diarrhoea. Earthworms are easily obtained and are of convenient size for dissections. They are, therefore, universally employed for class studies and for investigations in general and comparative physiology.

questions.text: Transfer of Taenia to secondary host occurs as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An oncosphere is the early larval stage of certain tapeworms. It is a free-swimming, ciliated structure that develops from the egg and is important in the life cycle of these parasitic organisms.

A cysticercus is the larval stage of certain tapeworms, particularly in the Taenia genus. It is a fluid-filled cyst containing the larval form known as a scolex. When ingested by a suitable host, the cysticercus develops into an adult tapeworm.

A morula is an early stage in the development of an embryo. It is a solid mass of cells resulting from the early divisions of a fertilized egg, prior to the formation of a blastocyst. The morula consists of tightly packed cells and is a transitional stage in embryonic development.
Egg is not a secondary host.

questions.explanation: Eggs of Taenia undergo cleavage to form morula. Morula, at its morphologically posterior end, develops three pairs of chitinous hooks secreted by differentiated cells, called onchoblasts. This six hooked embryo, called hexacanth, possesses a pair of large penetration glands. It is surrounded by two hexacanth membranes. The hexacanth, together with all the membranes surrounding it, is known as onchosphere. The secondary or intermediate host acquires infection by ingesting the onchospheres. Pig, which regularly feeds on human excreta is the usual secondary host, but dog, monkey and sheep are also known to get the infection. Man himself may serve as the secondary host by ingesting onchospheres with inadequately cooked or raw vegetables.

questions.text: Jelly fish belongs to the class _ :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrozoa is a class of marine animals belonging to the phylum Cnidaria. This class includes various organisms such as hydroids, fire corals, and the Portuguese man-of-war.

Jelly fish belongs to the Class Scyphozoa of the Phylum Cnidaria. Its genus is Aurelia. Aurelia is found in the coastal waters of the tropical and temperate seas. Aurelia may float passively or swim actively, singly or in shoals. It is carnivorous and unisexual. It has a soft, gelatinous, saucerlike body. Its margin bears numerous short tentacles and 8 sense organs, called rhopalia, in notches of the margin. Each sense organ is enclosed by a pair of leaflike lappets. At the centre of the ower (subumbrellar) surface is the squarish mouth surrounded by 4 long oral arms.

Anthozoa is a class of marine animals within the phylum Cnidaria. This class includes various sessile (non-motile) forms such as corals and sea anemones. Unlike the medusa form predominant in the Scyphozoa class (jellyfish), Anthozoa species are typically polyps throughout their lives.
Scyphozoa is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Jelly fish belongs to the Class Scyphozoa of the Phylum Cnidaria. Its genus is Aurelia. Aurelia is found in the coastal waters of the tropical and temperate seas. Aurelia may float passively or swim actively, singly or in shoals. It is carnivorous and unisexual. It has a soft, gelatinous, saucerlike body. Its margin bears numerous short tentacles and 8 sense organs, called rhopalia, in notches of the margin. Each sense organ is enclosed by a pair of leaflike lappets. At the centre of the ower (subumbrellar) surface is the squarish mouth surrounded by 4 long oral arms.

questions.text: The fish which can be used in biological control of mosquitoes/larvicidal fish is the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eels are not commonly used for biological control of mosquitoes. The fish species often employed for mosquito control, particularly in the larval stage, is Gambusia affinis, also known as mosquito fish.

While carp may consume mosquito larvae to some extent, they are not the most effective species for biological control of mosquitoes. The fish more commonly used for this purpose is Gambusia affinis, also known as mosquito fish.

The catfish is not typically used for biological control of mosquitoes. Instead, certain species of fish such as Gambusia affinis, commonly known as mosquito fish, are often used for larvicidal purposes.

The Gambusia is a species of freshwater fish. It is remarkably hardy, surviving in waters of very low oxygen saturations, high salinities and high temperatures. For these reasons, this species may now be the most widespread freshwater fish in the world, having being introduced as a biocontrol in certain countries to control mosquitoes. It feeds on larval and pupal stages of mosquitoes.

questions.explanation: The Gambusia is a species of freshwater fish. It is remarkably hardy, surviving in waters of very low oxygen saturations, high salinities and high temperatures. For these reasons, this species may now be the most widespread freshwater fish in the world, having being introduced as a biocontrol in certain countries to control mosquitoes. It feeds on larval and pupal stages of mosquitoes.

questions.text: Hair occur in all mammals except those of the order _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rodentia, the order of mammals that includes rodents, have hair. Rodents are mammals, and like all mammals except few, they are characterized by the presence of hair on their bodies. The type and color of the fur can vary among rodent species, but they do possess hair as a characteristic feature.
Chiroptera, the order of mammals that includes bats, have hair.
Primates, including humans, generally have hair on their bodies.

Order Cetacea includes whales, dolphins and porpoises. These are the most highly modified mammals. They have a fishlike body with smooth, hairless skin devoid of sweat and oil glands, far posterior nares, small eyes, minute ear openings without pinnae, paddlelike forelimbs, no hindlimbs, abdominal testes and a flattened tail ending in two horizontal flaps or flukes.

questions.explanation: Order Cetacea includes whales, dolphins and porpoises. These are the most highly modified mammals. questions.text: Bird vertebrae are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acoelous refers to vertebrae that are flat on both ends (mammals).
Bird vertebrae are heterocoelous, i.e. the centra of the vertebrae have saddle shaped ends.
Amphicoelous means both ends of the centrum are concave (fish).
Procoelous means concave in front and convex in back (anurans and reptiles).

questions.explanation: Bird vertebrae are heterocoelous, i.e. the centra of the vertebrae have saddle shaped ends. Acoelous refers to vertebrae that are flat on both ends (mammals). Amphicoelous means both ends of the centrum are concave (fish). Procoelous means concave in front and convex in back (anurans and reptiles).

questions.text: The feet of the kingfisher are modified for _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the hindlimbs of kingfishers are not specifically modified for wading.

The hindlimbs (or hind legs) of kingfishers are modified for perching and gripping onto branches. Kingfishers have zygodactyl feet, where two toes point forward and two toes point backward. This foot structure provides a strong and stable grip, allowing them to perch on branches, often near the water's edge, as they hunt for fish.
No, the hindlimbs of kingfishers are not adapted for running.
The hindlimbs of kingfishers are not specifically modified for catching prey. Instead, the adaptations in a kingfisher's hindlimbs are primarily related to perching and diving.

This foot structure provides a strong and stable grip, allowing them to perch on branches, often near the water's edge, as they hunt for fish.

questions.text: Both male and female pigeons secrete milk through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Male and female pigeons both produce a substance often referred to as "pigeon milk," which is a secretion from the lining of their crops (not salivary glands).
Pigeons produce "pigeon milk" through modified cells in their crop, not the adrenal glands.

Pigeons are noted for their unique ability to produce “pigeon’s milk”, a soft, cheesy and nourishing secretion, especially during the breeding season. It is formed by the degeneration of the epithelial cells lining the crop. It is regurgitated into the mouth of the young birds until they are old enough to manage a graindiet like their parents. The pigeon’s milk includes water, fat, protein (casein) and lactose. The milk is produced by both sexes and contains 35 percent of fat.

Both male and female pigeons secrete "pigeon milk" through a specialized tissue lining in their crop, not through the gizzard. The gizzard is a muscular part of a bird's stomach that aids in the mechanical breakdown of food.

The pigeon’s milk includes water, fat, protein (casein) and lactose. The milk is produced by both sexes and contains 35 percent of fat.

questions.text: The Typhlops is a _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sea snakes are a group of highly venomous snakes that have adapted to marine environments. They are often referred to simply as "sea snakes." These snakes are members of the subfamily Hydrophiinae, which is a part of the Elapidae family.

Glass snake is commonly used to describe a group of legless lizards rather than true snakes. Legless lizards are reptiles that resemble snakes but are distinguished by certain anatomical features, such as the presence of eyelids and external ear openings, which snakes lack.
Typhlops is a genus of blind snakes (non poisonous snakes) found in Europe, Africa, Asia and Central and South America.

The term "grass snake" typically refers to a non-venomous snake found in various parts of Europe and Asia. It is a common name for several species in the genus Natrix, with the European grass snake (Natrix natrix) being one of the most well-known.

questions.explanation: Typhlops is a genus of blind snakes (non poisonous snakes) found in Europe, Africa, Asia and Central and South America. Sea snakes are poisonous snakes while grass snakes are non poisonous. The Glass snake is a lizard.

questions.text: The necturus is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hell bender is a large salamander. Scientific name: Cryptobranchus alleganiensisIt is also a large aquatic salamander, but not in the genus Necturus.Hellbenders belong to a different family (Cryptobranchidae).They do not retain external gills as adults.

Amphiuma is a Congo Eel.Scientific name: AmphiumaAmphiumas are eel-like salamanders found in the southeastern U.S.They belong to the family Amphiumidae, not Proteidae.They have very small limbs and lack external gills as adults.
The necturus is a mud puppy belonging to the Order Urodela of Class Amphibia.
Ichthyophis is a blind worm. Commonly refers to legless lizards or sometimes caecilians.These are not salamanders at all.So, it is completely unrelated to Necturus.

questions.text: The Fire bellied toads are _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amphiuma refers to a genus of aquatic salamanders found in the southeastern United States.These salamanders are commonly known as amphiumas or congo eels.

The fire bellied toads are a group of eight species of small toads belonging to the genus Bombina. They are found across much of Europe and Asia, staying in water or near the shore. Their name is derived from the brightly coloured red or yellow and black patterns on their ventral region, which act as a warning to its predators.
Necturus is a mud puppy.
Salamandra refers to a genus of terrestrial or semi-aquatic salamanders.

questions.text: Which is not a true amphibian animal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amphibians constitute a class of cold-blooded vertebrates that includes salamanders, frogs, toads, and caecilians.
Amphibians constitute a class of cold-blooded vertebrates that includes salamanders, frogs, toads, and caecilians.
Tortoise belongs to the Class Reptilia. Its body is protected by a shell consisting of a dorsal carapace and ventral plastron.

questions.text: A wood boring mollusc/shipworm is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chitons are marine mollusks that belong to the class Polyplacophora.
The common name of Teredo is shipworm which belongs to the class bivalvia of the Phylum Mollusca. In it, the head is absent and the foot is wedge shaped for burrowing.
Limax refers to a genus of air-breathing land slugs. Slugs are gastropod mollusks that lack a protective external shell.

Patella, commonly known as limpets, belongs to the phylum Mollusca. Limpets are marine gastropod mollusks characterized by a conical shell that tightly adheres to rocks, and they are typically found in intertidal zones.

questions.explanation: The common name of Teredo is shipworm which belongs to the class bivalvia of the Phylum Mollusca. In it, the head is absent and the foot is wedge shaped for burrowing.

questions.text: Silk thread is obtained from the silk moth during the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The mulberry silk moth has been the most commercially important and beneficial insect. The silk is obtained by killing the pupa inside hot water and the silk thread is then wound.
Silk thread is obtained at pupal stage, not larval stage.
The silk moth (Bombyx mori) does not have a nymph stage.
After completing the pupal stage within the cocoon, the silk moth emerges as an adult.

questions.explanation: The mulberry silk moth has been the most commercially important and beneficial insect. The silk is obtained by killing the pupa inside hot water and the silk thread is then wound.

questions.text: The Organ Pipe Coral is a _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The common name of the Tubipora is Organ Pipe Coral. It is a marine animal of the Class Anthozoa (Phylum Cnidaria). It occurs on reefs in shallow waters of the Indian and Pacific oceans and is characterized by long, parallel upright polyps or stalks, supported by a skeleton of rigid tubes of calcium carbonate.
"Astraea" is used as a genus name for certain marine organisms. For example, Astraea snails are a type of reef-dwelling marine gastropod mollusk.

"Heliopora" is a genus of colonial marine organisms belonging to the phylum Cnidaria. Specifically, it is a type of blue coral. Blue corals are unique among corals because they have a skeleton made of aragonite, a form of calcium carbonate, and they can also produce a blue pigment.

"Fungia" refers to a genus of coral known as plate corals or mushroom corals. These corals are part of the family Fungiidae within the class Anthozoa. Fungia corals are solitary, free-living corals that do not form colonies like some other coral species. Instead, each individual coral is a single polyp that can grow to a large size.

questions.explanation: The common name of the Tubipora is Organ Pipe Coral. It is a marine animal of the Class Anthozoa (Phylum Cnidaria). It occurs on reefs in shallow waters of the Indian and Pacific oceans and is characterized by long, parallel upright polyps or stalks, supported by a skeleton of rigid tubes of calcium carbonate.

questions.text: The frog’s heart, when taken out of the body, continues to beat for sometime.Select the best reason for this from the following statements. (1) The frog is a poikilotherm. (2) The frog does not have any coronary circulation. (3) Its heart is “myogenic” in nature. (4) Its heart is autoexcitable.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: (3) is also correct because frog's heart is myogenic too.
Frog is homeotherm. Frog does have coronary circulation.

The frog’s heart is myogenic, i.e. its heartbeat originates from muscles of heart. Hence, it is autorythmic. A wave of contraction originates from the sinus venosus and spreads to the wall of sinus venosus and both auricles. This compels the heart to beat. Due to this reason, the frog’s heart will continue to beat as long as it gets a supply of ATP.
(4) is also correct beacuse frog's heart is autoexcitable.

questions.explanation: The frog’s heart is myogenic, i.e. its heartbeat originates from muscles of heart. Hence, it is autorythmic. A wave of contraction originates from the sinus venosus and spreads to the wall of sinus venosus and both auricles. This compels the heart to beat. Due to this reason, the frog’s heart will continue to beat as long as it gets a supply of ATP.

questions.text: Which type of tissue correctly matches with its location?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Transitional epithelium - tip of the nose
options.explanation: Tip of the nose has elastic cartilage.

options.text: Cuboidal epithelium - Lining of the stomach
options.explanation: Simple columnar epithelium lines the stomach.
Posterior part of the intestine has a single unit smooth muscle in which all fibres of the muscle contract simultaneously as a single unit.

options.text: Areolar tissue - Tendons of the nose
options.explanation: Areolar tissue is a type of loose connective tissue that provides support to organs and connects different tissues. Tendons, on the other hand, are dense connective tissues that connect muscles to bones. The tendons of the nose are not primarily composed of areolar tissue but rather consist of fibrous connective tissue.

questions.explanation: Posterior part of the intestine has a single unit smooth muscle in which all fibres of the muscle contract simultaneously as a single unit.

questions.text: The body cells in cockroach discharge their nitrogenous waste in the haemolymph mainly in the form of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nitrogenous waste is primarily discharged in the form of urea in mammals, including humans. Uric acid is a common nitrogenous waste product in insects.
In cockroaches, body cells discharge their nitrogenous waste into the hemolymph primarily in the form of uric acid or ammonia, not calcium carbonate.
Nitrogenous waste is typically discharged in the form of ammonia in aquatic animals, particularly in fish and many aquatic invertebrates. Uric acid is a common nitrogenous waste product in insects.

In cockroaches, Malpighian tubules extract metabolic wastes like potassium and sodium urate, water and carbon dioxide from the blood. In the Malpighian tubules, bicarbonates of potassium and sodium, water and uric acid are formed. A large amount of water and bicarbonates of potassium and sodium are reabsorbed by the cells of Malpighian tubules and then transferred to the blood (haemolymph). Uric acid is carried to the alimentary canal of the insect and is finally passed out through anus.

questions.explanation: In cockroaches, Malpighian tubules extract metabolic wastes like potassium and sodium urate, water and carbon dioxide from the blood. In the Malpighian tubules, bicarbonates of potassium and sodium, water and uric acid are formed. A large amount of water and bicarbonates of potassium and sodium are reabsorbed by the cells of Malpighian tubules and then transferred to the blood (haemolymph). Uric acid is carried to the alimentary canal of the insect and is finally passed out through anus.

questions.text: The terga, sterna and pleura of the cockroach's body are joined by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The arthrodial membrane is a tough, flexible cuticle that joins the skeletal elements of cockroach and other arthropods. It connects terga, pleura and sterna of the cockroach's body.

Cockroaches do not have cartilage. Cartilage is a connective tissue that is found in vertebrates, particularly in the skeletons of mammals. Cockroaches are insects, and their skeletal structure is external and made of a tough, flexible substance called chitin.

Cockroaches, like many insects, produce secretions that can serve as a type of cementing glue, but the terminology may vary. These secretions are often used for various purposes such as building nests, attaching egg cases (oothecae) to surfaces, or even securing themselves in sheltered locations.

In cockroaches and other insects, the terga, sterna, and pleura are parts of the exoskeleton, which is the external skeletal structure. These components are not directly joined by muscular tissue; instead, they are connected by arthrodial membrane.

questions.explanation: The arthrodial membrane is a tough, flexible cuticle that joins the skeletal elements of cockroach and other arthropods. It connects terga, pleura and sterna of the cockroach's body.

questions.text: What external changes are visible after the last moult of a cockroach nymph?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cockroaches undergo paurometabolous development. The nymph moults about 67 times to reach the adult form. The next to last nymphal stage has wing pads but only adult cockroaches have wings.

After the last molt of a cockroach nymph, external changes include the development of wings, reproductive organs, and the attainment of adult characteristics. However, the labium, which is a part of the mouthparts, is already present and functional in the nymphal stages.
The hardness of the mandibles, which are a pair of powerful jaws used for chewing, doesn't typically change drastically after the final molt.
